reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Opens with the mandatory Preview Warning callout as required by section instructions, setting correct expectations before any other content.

All six clients are accurately documented with correct config paths, root keys, and JSON/TOML formats verified against the source — including the VS Code-specific 'servers' root key (not 'mcpServers'), and the OpenCode 'local' type variant.
Tool parameter tables for all four tools are complete and grounded in the source, including the correct default values (maxItems: 3, logs limit: 50, dev mode agentPort: 7331).
The tabbed manual-setup section gives first-time users a copy-paste config for all six clients in one compact block — the most valuable single section on the page for a beginner.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-service use-case, and comparison) and correctly uses 10 Q&A pairs.
The dev mode 12-minute timeout and the deploy/delete autoConfirmOperation injection are both correctly sourced.
Secret masking behavior in secret_get responses is called out explicitly — important security context a first-time user wouldn't expect.
Troubleshooting section covers realistic first-time-setup failures (PATH, authentication, JSON parse errors) rather than theoretical edge cases.
problems: Inaccurate detection mechanism description: the page says mcp:add 'detects which clients are installed', but the source (detectTarget) checks only whether config file paths already exist on disk — the application itself is never probed. A user with Claude Code installed but no existing .mcp.json or ~/.claude.json would not be detected.

Unsupported environment variable claim: the Troubleshooting section states 'You can override the CLI command the MCP server uses internally by setting the STACKTAPE_MCP_CLI_COMMAND environment variable.' This variable does not appear anywhere in the provided source files (mcp/index.ts or mcp-add/index.ts) and may be invented.

Unsupported timeout claim in FAQ: 'Infrastructure operations have a default timeout of 10 minutes.' The only timeout explicitly in the source is the dev mode 12-minute timeout (timeoutMs: 12 * 60 * 1000). No per-operation timeout is defined in the provided source.

The <FlowDiagram steps={[...]} /> component in the 'How it works' section is not listed in the style guide or confirmed in the documented MDX component palette. If the component does not exist in the Stacktape docs renderer, this section will silently fail to render.

The autoConfirmOperation behavior description is slightly off: the source shows that when no clients are detected, getDefaults falls back to ALL target IDs — not just detected ones. The page implies it always installs into detected clients only.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the detection mechanism description: replace 'detects which clients are installed' with 'detects clients that have an existing config file at one of the known paths'. Add a clarifying note that a newly installed client with no prior config will not be marked as detected, but can still be selected from the list.

Remove the STACKTAPE_MCP_CLI_COMMAND troubleshooting item entirely until it is confirmed in source. If the env var does exist but was missing from the provided files, add a source citation. Do not publish an unsupported environment variable override that users will attempt to set and find does nothing.

Remove or replace the '10 minutes' ops timeout claim in the FAQ. If the real timeout is unknown from the available source, write 'Infrastructure operations run as subprocesses; if a deployment exceeds its timeout, the MCP server terminates the subprocess and returns an error. Run long deployments directly via the CLI for more control.' — removing the specific number.

Replace <FlowDiagram steps={[...]} /> with either a prose paragraph or a markdown ordered list. The component is not confirmed in the MDX palette and risks rendering as nothing. The content is clear enough as prose and doesn't need a diagram component.
optionalImprovements: Tighten the autoConfirmOperation description: note that if no clients are detected (no existing config files), the command defaults to offering all supported clients rather than stopping or producing an empty selection.

The 'When to use' section is slightly generic and reachable only by a user who already knows they want MCP. Consider leading with the friction it removes ('instead of running stacktape deploy in a terminal, your AI assistant can do it directly') before the bullet list.

The 'How it works' section could note that the lexical index is built once at server startup from bundled documentation files — not rebuilt on each query — which is relevant to users wondering about startup latency.

Consider a one-sentence note in the Supported Clients table or Automatic Setup section clarifying that detection is config-file-based, not application-based, so newly installed clients may need manual selection.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: Draft characterizes `stacktape_diagnose` as "read-only" in two places: the tool section heading ("Inspects deployed infrastructure with read-only operations") and the FAQ ("the `stacktape_docs` and `stacktape_diagnose` tools are read-only").
issues.reason: The diagnose tool includes `container_exec` (runs arbitrary commands in containers), `sql` (can execute INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DDL), `dynamodb`/`redis`/`opensearch` (accept `operation`+`item`/`value`/`document` parameters for writes), and `aws_sdk` (calls any AWS API). These are clearly not read-only. Calling the tool read-only is a safety mischaracterization — a reader who trusts this claim may auto-approve all diagnose tool calls without review.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the "read-only" qualifier from both locations. In the tool section: change to "Inspects and interacts with deployed infrastructure" or similar. In the FAQ: change to "While `stacktape_docs` is read-only, `stacktape_diagnose` can also execute commands in containers and run database queries — review its tool calls before approving."

issues.statement: "When a project-level config file already exists, `mcp:add` writes there. Otherwise it falls back to the global config path."
issues.reason: The `resolveTargetPath` function returns the first existing path in the `paths` array, or `paths[0]` if none exist. Since `paths[0]` is always the project-level path for every target, when NO config file exists, `mcp:add` creates the config at the project level — it does NOT fall back to the global path. The draft's claim inverts the actual default behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with: "If a project-level or global config file already exists, `mcp:add` writes to the first one it finds (checking project-level first). If neither exists, it creates a new project-level config file."

issues.statement: "If you pass `--autoConfirmOperation`, the command skips the prompt and installs into all detected clients."
issues.reason: The `getDefaults` function returns `detectedTargets` only when at least one client is detected. When NO clients are detected, it returns `allTargets` (all six clients). So `--autoConfirmOperation` installs into all six clients when none are detected, not just detected ones. The draft omits this edge case which could surprise users.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "If you pass `--autoConfirmOperation`, the command skips the prompt. It installs into all detected clients, or all six supported clients if none are detected."

issues.statement: The stacktape_diagnose section says "Database query operations (`sql`, `dynamodb`, `redis`, `opensearch`) require both `resourceName` and the query/operation parameters."
issues.reason: This is accurate for `sql` (requires `resourceName` + `sql`), but for `dynamodb`, `redis`, and `opensearch` the source requires `resourceName` + `operation` — not a generic "query/operation parameters". The parameter name `operation` is specific and important since it determines whether the call reads or writes data. The draft's table correctly lists the parameters above, but this summary sentence blurs the distinction.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "Database operations (`sql`, `dynamodb`, `redis`, `opensearch`) require `resourceName` and a command parameter — `sql` for SQL queries, `operation` for NoSQL/cache/search operations."

issues.statement: The draft does not mention that the diagnose tool's `logs` operation requires `resourceName` as a mandatory parameter.
issues.reason: The parameter table for stacktape_diagnose says "Most operations need `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, and `resourceName`" without distinguishing which are validated as required. The source shows `logs` explicitly validates `resourceName` as required, as do `metrics`, `alarms`, `container_exec`, `sql`, and the NoSQL operations. This is important because the AI assistant will get a validation error if it omits `resourceName` for logs.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a note under the parameter table or in the `logs` description that `resourceName` is required for log queries, not just recommended.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall. Tool operations, parameter schemas, client configurations, and behavioral details (timeouts, defaults, masking, truncation) are accurately represented against the MCP source code. The most significant issue is the characterization of `stacktape_diagnose` as "read-only" in two locations — the tool includes container_exec (arbitrary command execution), sql (arbitrary SQL), and write-capable NoSQL/cache operations, making this a safety mischaracterization. The config path fallback behavior is also inverted: when no config exists, mcp:add creates at project level, not global. A minor edge case with --autoConfirmOperation behavior is also slightly misrepresented. All manual setup snippets, tool parameter tables, and FAQ answers (including BM25 details, timeout values, and env var override) checked out against source.

The draft is broadly aligned with the MCP command source and includes the required preview framing, but it has several source-grounding issues around mcp:add installation behavior, safety/read-only wording, docs-search internals, secret masking, timeouts, and unsupported security/configuration claims.

issues.statement: "When a project-level config file already exists, `mcp:add` writes there. Otherwise it falls back to the global config path."
issues.reason: The installer writes to the first existing candidate path. If none of the candidate config files exists, it creates the first path in the target list, which is the project-level path for these clients, not the global path.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with: "`mcp:add` writes to the first existing config path in the target list. If no config file exists yet, it creates the project-level config path for that client."

issues.statement: The draft says `mcp:add` "auto-detects installed clients" and that `--autoConfirmOperation` "installs into all detected clients."
issues.reason: The source detects existing config files, not installed applications. Also, when no config files are detected, the default selected targets are all supported clients, so `--autoConfirmOperation` can install into all targets, not only detected ones.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that `mcp:add` detects existing client config files. Clarify that if no existing config files are found, the command defaults to all supported clients when prompting is skipped.

issues.statement: The draft describes `stacktape_diagnose` as read-only, including the FAQ statement that `stacktape_docs` and `stacktape_diagnose` are read-only tools.
issues.reason: The MCP source labels the diagnose tool as intended for inspection, but the runtime surface includes `container_exec` and `aws_sdk` with arbitrary command-style inputs. The MCP layer shown here does not enforce read-only AWS SDK commands or container commands, so the page should avoid promising read-only behavior without a caveat.
issues.suggestedFix: Write that `stacktape_diagnose` is intended for diagnostics and includes log, metric, database, container exec, and AWS SDK operations. Add that users should review tool calls carefully, especially `container_exec` and `aws_sdk`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly complete for a setup page: it names the preview status, supported clients, install paths, four MCP tools, operation unions, stdio transport, and key safety caveats. I found a few medium-impact API completeness gaps where documented tool parameters or installer behavior would materially help users operate the MCP server correctly.

issues.statement: The `stacktape_diagnose` section lists only `operation` and a generic `args` object, then says most operations need `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, and `resourceName`.
issues.reason: The diagnose tool has per-operation required arguments and allowed arguments that are important for common workflows. The draft mentions some requirements in prose, but omits required `metric` for metrics, required `command` for container_exec, required `sql` for sql, required `operation` for DynamoDB/Redis/OpenSearch, and required `service`/`command` for aws_sdk. Readers cannot reliably construct common diagnostic calls from the page.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a compact per-operation table for `stacktape_diagnose` that names each operation and its required arguments, with optional filters summarized separately.

issues.statement: The `stacktape_ops` section says most operations require `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, and `configPath`, and documents special requirements only for secret operations.
issues.reason: The source validates `script_run` with `scriptName` as a required argument. Because running deployment scripts is one of the named operations, omitting `scriptName` from the operation-specific requirements makes the API guidance incomplete.
issues.suggestedFix: Add `script_run` to the special-case requirements: `script_run` requires `scriptName`; `secret_create` requires `secretName` plus `secretValue` or `secretFile`; `secret_get` and `secret_delete` require `secretName`.

issues.statement: The page does not document that MCP tool arguments may be passed in snake_case or kebab-case forms and are normalized to camelCase.
issues.reason: The MCP server includes explicit argument aliases and a camel-case normalizer. This is a practical API behavior for AI clients because model-generated tool calls often use `project_name`, `config_path`, or `resource-name` style names. Documenting it reduces avoidable tool-call failures.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short note under Available tools: argument names are normalized to camelCase, and common aliases such as `project_name`, `config_path`, `resource_name`, `secret_name`, and `auto_confirm_operation` are accepted.

positiveFindings: The draft starts with the required preview warning and avoids promising production-ready MCP behavior.
All four exposed MCP tools are named, and each operation union is acknowledged for `stacktape_ops`, `stacktape_diagnose`, and `stacktape_dev`.
The manual setup examples are demonstrative for the setup task and match the client-specific config shapes in the installer source.
The draft correctly documents destructive delete confirmation and masking of `secret_get` responses.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No style violations: no hand-authored YAML Stacktape configs, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig — the page correctly uses JSON fenced blocks only for MCP client config files, not Stacktape configs.

All four tools are documented with accurate parameter tables sourced directly from the MCP server code — operation enums, required args, and defaults (50-entry log limit, 7331 dev port, 30,000-char truncation) all match the source.

Per-client manual setup examples in tabbed layout are copy-pasteable and technically accurate against the source's serverConfig objects — including the VS Code 'servers' root key distinction and the OpenCode 'local' type.
The `stacktape mcp:add` detection and auto-confirm behavior is accurately described: detected-first defaulting, `.bak.<timestamp>` backups, JSON5 parsing — all match the source code.
Warning callout for container_exec and aws_sdk being non-read-only is well-placed and important for beginners who might assume the diagnose tool is entirely passive.
Preview callout at the top satisfies the section instruction requirement and correctly sets expectations without overstating stability.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (authentication, credential handling, scoping), AWS service use-case (production safety, concurrent usage), and comparison/choosing questions (ops vs diagnose distinction). Exactly 10 Q&A pairs.
Troubleshooting section addresses the three most common real-world failure modes (PATH, auth, config syntax) grounded in the actual install flow.
problems: Missing a critical first-time-user gotcha: after running `stacktape mcp:add` or editing config files manually, the user must restart their AI client for the new MCP server to be loaded. This is the single most common reason setup 'doesn't work' and is completely absent from the page, the troubleshooting section, and the FAQ.

The 'Per-operation required arguments' table for stacktape_diagnose shows 'info_stack' with '—' in the Required args column, implying no arguments are needed. In practice, projectName, stage, and region are necessary to identify which stack to inspect — the source's DIAGNOSE_ALLOWED_ARGS confirms these are accepted. A reader copying the table literally may call the operation with no args and get an empty/misleading result.

The 'Available tools' section introduces stacktape_diagnose as 'inspects and interacts' but a few paragraphs later the Warning callout says 'most operations are read-only'. These two framings conflict slightly. The Warning is accurate; the intro framing should lead with the read-mostly nature upfront.

The 'When to use' section lists four bullets that are narrowly tool-specific (deploying, querying, docs lookup, secrets) but doesn't communicate the core value proposition in one sentence: that an AI assistant can drive the entire Stacktape workflow without the user needing to relay CLI output manually. A first-time reader may not immediately understand what they gain versus just asking the AI to write CLI commands for them.

The 'Verifying the setup' section tells users to run `stacktape mcp` and press Ctrl+C, but the output ('wait for input on stdin') is not what most clients produce — clients wrap the process and the user never sees stdin. This step is confusing in practice. The more useful verification is the docs-query test mentioned in the next paragraph, which should be primary.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence note after the setup sections (both Automatic and Manual) telling the reader to restart their AI client before the MCP server will appear. Place it in a short Info callout: 'After editing any MCP config file, restart your AI client to load the new server.' This alone will prevent the most common support question.

Fix the info_stack row in the diagnose required-args table. Change '—' to 'projectName, stage, region (to identify the stack)' or add a table note explaining that stack-identifying args are needed even when not validation-enforced.

Reorder the 'Verifying the setup' section: lead with the in-client docs-query test ('try asking your AI assistant: how do I deploy a Lambda function') as the primary verification, and demote the `stacktape mcp` terminal test to a secondary step for debugging server startup issues only.

Add a sentence to the 'When to use' intro naming the core workflow benefit explicitly: something like 'The assistant can then drive the full Stacktape workflow — write config, deploy, check logs, iterate — without you relaying terminal output back and forth.'

The stacktape_diagnose intro says 'inspects and interacts' — change to 'primarily reads deployed infrastructure' to align with the Warning callout and reduce the mild framing conflict. Then the Warning can refine with the two exceptions (container_exec, aws_sdk).

Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.

The VS Code global config path is described as 'Platform-specific User/mcp.json' — the Windows path (%APPDATA%/Code/User/mcp.json) could be called out explicitly given this documentation targets a Windows dev environment

The 'How it works' section could note that info_stacks receives special response shaping (stack lines extracted from outputEvents, rawTail stripped) since this affects programmatic response parsing for developers building tooling on top of MCP

The Troubleshooting section could add a note about the agent mode behavior (isAgentMode detection) — when the MCP server calls back into itself via shell, it may be detected as agent mode and skip prompts, which can surprise developers debugging MCP-initiated commands

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Preview Warning callout appears immediately, setting correct expectations before the reader absorbs any technical details — exactly right for a feature in active development.

All six supported clients are covered with both a reference table and copy-paste-ready JSON/TOML config snippets using <Tabs>, giving the reader exactly what they need to configure their specific editor.
Parameter tables for all four tools are dense and well-structured — query, type, description per row — making them extractable as standalone reference snippets.
Per-operation required-args tables for stacktape_diagnose are unusually thorough; readers can cross-check what's needed without reading source code.

Security nuances are correctly scoped: the Warning callout inside stacktape_diagnose names specifically container_exec and aws_sdk as the non-read-only outliers rather than blanket-claiming the whole tool is safe or dangerous.
The 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use' sections are genuinely opinionated — 'long-running deployments are better run directly via the CLI' is concrete and actionable, not circular.
FAQ is exactly 10 questions, mixing Stacktape-specific (credentials, scoping, ops-vs-diagnose), technology-background (what is MCP), and practical-outcome questions (timeouts, alongside CLI usage).

The mcp:add detection/auto-selection behavior (defaults to detected clients, falls back to all six if none detected, skips prompt under --autoConfirmOperation) is explained clearly and matches the source precisely.
The node_modules/.bin/stacktape version-pinning behavior for project-level configs is explained in both automatic and manual setup sections — a subtle but important detail for npm-based projects.
Troubleshooting subsections use specific H3 labels ('Server fails to start', 'Config file conflicts', etc.) that make them individually extractable by search engines and AI overviews.
problems: The stacktape_docs 'mode' parameter table lists the three values (answer, reference, snippet) with brief descriptions, but no example of when a reader would choose snippet over answer — the section stops after the table without prose explaining the practical difference between modes, leaving tunable values partially unexplained.

The FAQ skips a direct choosing/comparison question (MCP server vs. CLI vs. Console — when to use which?) even though 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' cover some of this. A well-formed comparison question would be cited by AI engines answering 'should I use MCP or CLI for Stacktape deployments?'

The 'How it works' section's numbered list largely restates what earlier sections already explained (four tools, stdio transport, local docs index, JSONL subprocess). The response-truncation detail (30,000 characters) is the only net-new fact — the rest of the section could be tightened without losing coverage.

The VS Code global config path is described as 'Platform-specific User/mcp.json' in the table without spelling out the Windows or macOS paths, even though the source enumerates them explicitly. Readers on Windows seeing %APPDATA%\Code\User\mcp.json in their filesystem have no breadcrumb to connect to the documentation.

The stacktape_dev section doesn't state what happens to activeDevSession state if the AI client process restarts mid-session — the module-level singleton is process-scoped, so a client restart loses the session reference and requires a new 'start'. This is a gotcha readers will encounter.

The Info callout at the bottom of stacktape_diagnose ('Secret operations are handled by stacktape_ops, not stacktape_diagnose') repeats information from the tool description table immediately above it — minor padding that slightly weakens the section's density signal.
optionalImprovements: Add 1-2 sentences after the stacktape_docs mode parameter table explaining when to use 'snippet' (e.g., when the AI needs a TypeScript config block to inline) versus 'answer' (full explanation) — this anchors the tunable value for readers.

Add a FAQ question: 'MCP server, CLI, or Stacktape Console — which should I use?' with a 2-3 sentence answer covering the three paths. This maps to a high-intent comparison query that AI engines will cite.

In the VS Code row of the Supported clients table, spell out the platform-specific global path (e.g., %APPDATA%\Code\User\mcp.json on Windows, ~/Library/Application Support/Code/User/mcp.json on macOS) rather than 'Platform-specific User/mcp.json'.
Add a one-sentence note in the stacktape_dev section explaining that the active session reference is process-scoped: if the AI client restarts, the session is lost and 'start' must be called again.

Trim the 'How it works' numbered list to the two genuinely non-obvious facts: (1) that JSONL event streams are parsed and consolidated before the response is returned, and (2) the 30,000-character truncation limit. Remove the restatements of the tool count and transport type already covered in the introduction.

Replace the redundant Info callout at the bottom of the stacktape_diagnose section with a cross-link anchor (e.g., 'See stacktape_ops above for secret management') to avoid padding without losing the navigation cue.

issues.statement: FAQ answer 'What happens if a deployment times out through MCP?' says 'The dev mode start operation has a 12-minute timeout for initial setup' but does not mention the 10-minute default timeout for all other infrastructure operations (deploy, delete, preview_changes, etc.).
issues.reason: The cli-jsonl-runner.ts file shows a default timeout of 10 * 60 * 1000 ms (10 minutes) for all operations. Dev start overrides this to 12 minutes. The FAQ only mentions the 12-minute dev timeout, leaving readers unaware that deploy/delete/rollback also have a 10-minute MCP-layer timeout. A user deploying a large stack through MCP could hit this limit unexpectedly.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence to the FAQ answer: 'All other infrastructure operations (deploy, delete, preview, rollback, etc.) have a default 10-minute timeout.' Also consider mentioning this in the stacktape_ops section.

evidence.quote: timeoutMs = 10 * 60 * 1000
timeoutMs: 12 * 60 * 1000

issues.statement: The 'When NOT to use' section says 'you get real-time streaming output rather than a consolidated JSON response' when running via CLI directly.
issues.reason: While the contrast between CLI streaming and MCP consolidated responses is directionally correct, it understates the practical concern. The real reason to prefer CLI for long deployments is the 10-minute MCP timeout, not just the output format. A deployment that takes 15 minutes will succeed via CLI but fail via MCP.
issues.suggestedFix: Mention the MCP-layer timeout as the primary reason to prefer CLI for long-running deployments: 'Long-running deployments (over 10 minutes) should be run directly via the CLI, which has no MCP-layer timeout and provides real-time streaming output.'

verifierResults.summary: This is a high-quality, well-grounded draft. Nearly every factual claim — tool parameters, operation maps, validation logic, client config paths, default values, and behavioral descriptions — directly matches the source code. The only substantive issue is a missing mention of the 10-minute default timeout for non-dev infrastructure operations (deploy, delete, rollback, etc.), which the FAQ partially addresses for dev-start (12 minutes) but omits for the general case. This could surprise users whose deployments exceed 10 minutes when run through MCP.

The draft is mostly well grounded in the two MCP source files, especially around supported clients, install paths, tool names, operation maps, and safety behavior. I found a few concrete issues where the prose overstates behavior or describes argument requirements more broadly than the implementation supports.

issues.statement: “Most operations require `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, and `configPath`.”
issues.reason: The `stacktape_ops` MCP tool description says most operations require these arguments, but the implementation only validates extra required arguments for `script_run`, `secret_create`, `secret_get`, and `secret_delete`. It does not enforce `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, or `configPath` for deploy, delete, preview, rollback, or compile-template at the MCP layer. The draft turns a broad tool description into a stronger requirement than the runtime validation supports.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording: “For project-scoped operations, pass the same arguments the corresponding CLI command needs, commonly `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, and `configPath`. The MCP layer adds explicit validation for script and secret operations.”

evidence.quote:
Required args for most operations: projectName, stage, region, configPath.
Secret operations require: secretName (and secretValue or secretFile for secret_create).

issues.statement: “`info_stack` | — | View details of a deployed stack” in the `stacktape_diagnose` required-arguments table.
issues.reason: The implementation does not validate a required argument for `info_stack`, but it explicitly allows `stackName`, `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, `awsAccount`, and `logLevel`. Saying there are no required args is potentially misleading because the underlying CLI likely needs enough information to identify a stack; the source only supports saying the MCP wrapper does not enforce required args for this operation.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the table entry to something like: “MCP wrapper: none enforced. Pass `stackName` or project/stage/region details as needed by `stacktape info:stack`.”

evidence.quote: info_stack: ['stackName', 'projectName', 'stage', 'region', 'awsAccount', 'logLevel'],

issues.statement: “Common optional filters include `startTime`, `endTime`, `limit`, `filter`, and `query` for log operations; `period` and `stat` for metrics; `container` and `taskArn` for container exec; and `bastionResource` for SQL queries...”
issues.reason: The source allows `query` for `logs`, but it does not allow `query` for the `sql` operation. Because this sentence can be read as describing query-like arguments across diagnostic operations, it should be clearer that SQL uses the required `sql` argument, while `query` is only an allowed argument for logs and OpenSearch.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite as separate bullets per operation: logs accepts `startTime`, `endTime`, `limit`, `filter`, `container`, and `query`; SQL requires `sql` and can also accept `limit`, `timeout`, and `bastionResource`; OpenSearch accepts `query`.

issues.reason: The source confirms `stacktape mcp` uses `StdioServerTransport`, but it does not document terminal behavior such as visibly waiting for stdin or `Ctrl+C` as the stop mechanism. issues.statement: “After upgrading, you do not need to re-run `mcp:add` — the existing config points to the `stacktape mcp` command, which picks up the new version.”
issues.reason: The provided source shows that `mcp:add` can write either the global `stacktape` command or `node_modules/.bin/stacktape` for local project configs. Whether an existing config picks up a new version depends on how Stacktape was installed and updated. The source does not support the unconditional “do not need to re-run” statement.
issues.suggestedFix: Qualify the claim: “If the MCP config still points at the Stacktape binary you updated, the next client launch uses that updated binary. Re-run `stacktape mcp:add` only if you want to change which clients or config files are wired.”

issues.statement: “The `stacktape_ops` tool can deploy and delete real infrastructure; destructive operations like `delete` require explicit confirmation (`confirm: true`).”
issues.reason: This is true for the MCP wrapper, but the phrasing “destructive operations like `delete`” may imply there are other destructive operations with the same `confirm: true` MCP safety gate. The source only applies that gate to `delete`.
issues.suggestedFix: Say exactly: “The MCP wrapper requires `confirm: true` only for the `delete` operation.”

verifierResults.summary: The draft is generally complete for a non-resource MCP setup page: it covers the supported clients, automatic/manual setup, all four MCP tools, core operation sets, safety notes, troubleshooting, and exactly 10 FAQ entries. I found a few completeness and API-shape issues that could mislead readers configuring or operating the preview server.

issues.statement: The `stacktape_ops` section says most operations require `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, and `configPath`, but it does not mention that the tool normalizes snake_case and kebab-case argument names to camelCase.
issues.reason: The MCP tool accepts `project_name`, `config_path`, `secret_name`, `start_time`, and similar aliases, then normalizes keys before validation and CLI execution. This is useful API behavior for AI clients and manual tool-call debugging, and the page currently presents only camelCase names.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short note under Available tools or each parameter table: `MCP tool arguments are normalized before execution, so common snake_case and kebab-case forms such as project_name, config_path, secret_name, start_time, and end_time are accepted and mapped to camelCase.`

issues.statement: The `stacktape_diagnose` section lists optional arguments but does not explain that unsupported arguments are silently filtered per operation before the CLI command runs.
issues.reason: The source explicitly whitelists allowed args per diagnose operation and drops anything not listed. That behavior matters because users and AI clients may expect arbitrary CLI flags to pass through, especially for `aws_sdk`, database, and logs operations.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a caveat after the diagnose parameter table: `For diagnose operations, the MCP server forwards only the allowlisted arguments for the selected operation; extra keys are ignored instead of being passed through to the CLI.`

evidence.quote: const filteredArgs = pickAllowedArgs(normalizedArgs, DIAGNOSE_ALLOWED_ARGS[operation]);

issues.statement: The draft documents `stacktape_dev` as one active dev session per MCP server process, but does not document the `NOT_FOUND` behavior for status, logs, rebuild, rebuild_all, or stop before a session is started or after the local agent becomes unreachable.
issues.reason: The source has explicit behavior for missing or stale dev sessions, including next actions. This is important because most dev-mode operations depend on a prior successful `start`, and users troubleshooting AI client calls need to understand that session state is held inside the MCP server process.
issues.suggestedFix: In the dev-mode section or troubleshooting section, add: `Operations other than start require an active dev session in the same MCP server process. If no session is active, or the recorded local agent port no longer responds to /health, the tool returns NOT_FOUND and asks the assistant to call start again.`

message: `Dev session on port ${stalePort} is no longer reachable.`,
        nextActions: ['Call stacktape_dev with operation=start to create a new dev session']

issues.statement: The FAQ says `No credentials are sent to third parties beyond what normal CLI operations require (AWS API calls, Stacktape API for login).`
issues.reason: The provided source confirms local subprocess execution, secret masking for `secret_get`, and use of Stacktape CLI commands, but it does not establish every outbound credential path or third-party transmission behavior. As written, this is a broad security guarantee beyond the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the FAQ answer to source-grounded behavior: `The MCP server runs locally as a subprocess of your AI client and delegates operations to Stacktape CLI commands. It uses the same local authentication context those CLI commands use. The source also masks secret_get data in MCP responses.` Avoid the third-party guarantee unless a higher-priority source explicitly documents it.

issues.statement: The FAQ says `After upgrading, you do not need to re-run mcp:add — the existing config points to the stacktape mcp command, which picks up the new version.`
issues.reason: This is only clearly true when the installed client config points to a command path that resolves to the upgraded CLI. The installer may write `node_modules/.bin/stacktape` for project-level configs when the project has a Stacktape npm dependency, so a global `stacktape upgrade` would not update that local dependency path.
issues.suggestedFix: Qualify the update guidance: `You usually do not need to re-run mcp:add after upgrading the CLI path referenced by your MCP config. If the config points to node_modules/.bin/stacktape, update the project dependency; if it points to stacktape, update the global CLI.`

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
